steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Textual) question. The claim states that Toomer uses figurative language to connect Washington, DC, and the rural South. The correct quotation must do two things: (1) include figurative language, and (2) explicitly bring together both the urban DC setting and the rural Southern past.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "The Capitol dome looked like a gray ghost ship drifting in from sea.": Contains figurative language (simile), but it describes only Washington, DC. There is no reference to the rural South. ✗
  • Choice B — "The [train] engines of this valley have a whistle, the echoes of which sound like iterated gasps and sobs. I always think of them as crude music.": Uses figurative language, but the setting is a valley — neither DC nor the South is named, so the geographic connection is absent. ✗
  • Choice C — "And when the wind is from the South, soil of my homeland falls like a fertile shower upon the lean streets of [Washington, DC].": Uses a simile ("falls like a fertile shower") and explicitly names both the South ("soil of my homeland") and Washington, DC. This is figurative language bridging both places. ✓
  • Choice D — "The young trees had not outgrown their [planter] boxes then. V Street [in Washington, DC] was lined with them.": Set entirely in Washington, DC with no figurative language and no reference to the rural South. ✗
C is correct.
